title: Reproduce and fix OCCT STEP export regressions
analysis: Four handle.rs STEP export tests fail when run in parallel but pass with --test-threads=1: export_step_contains_iso_header, export_async_returns_step_bytes, multi_operation_sequence, and async_multi_op_sequence. All fail with 'STEPControl_Writer::Write failed' or 'OCCT export_step: StepSelect_StepType / InterfaceModel : AddWithRefs'. Root cause: OCCT's STEPControl_Writer (and Transfer/Write pipeline) uses process-global state (XSAlgo session, interface model, shape naming tables) that is not thread-safe. Each test spawns its own OcctKernelHandle with its own dedicated OS thread, but when multiple kernel threads call export_step() concurrently, they collide on OCCT's global STEP writer state. The fix is a process-wide C++ mutex around the entire export_step() function body. This serializes STEP exports across all kernel threads without changing the Rust API or the dedicated-thread architecture. The mutex lives in the C++ wrapper because that is where the OCCT global state contention happens — it is the narrowest correct scope. Lib.rs tests all pass because OcctKernel is used single-threaded (no dedicated thread spawning). Additionally, we should add a focused regression test that explicitly spawns multiple OcctKernelHandle instances and exports concurrently, to ensure parallel export is safe going forward.

steps.description: Write a failing test in handle.rs that explicitly reproduces the parallel STEP export race: spawn N (e.g. 4) OcctKernelHandle instances each on their own thread, each creates a box and exports to STEP concurrently via std::thread::scope, asserts all exports contain ISO-10303-21 header. This test should fail reliably under the current code because multiple kernel threads hit export_step() simultaneously.

Add a process-global std::mutex in occt_wrapper.cpp that guards the entire export_step() function body. Wrap the existing try block contents (STEPControl_Writer creation, Transfer, mkstemp, Write, file read, cleanup) inside a std::lock_guard<std::mutex> using a file-static mutex. This serializes all concurrent STEP export calls across all kernel threads. Add #include <mutex> to the includes.

Verify the new concurrent_export_step_from_multiple_handles test now passes, and re-run the full handle.rs test suite (cargo test -p reify-kernel-occt) to confirm all 4 previously-failing tests (export_step_contains_iso_header, export_async_returns_step_bytes, multi_operation_sequence, async_multi_op_sequence) now pass in parallel mode.

Run the full workspace test suite (cargo test --workspace or at least the crates that use STEP export: reify-kernel-occt, reify-eval, reify-geometry) to ensure no regressions elsewhere. The eval boundary/e2e tests that do STEP export should also pass.

design_decisions.decision: Place the mutex in C++ (occt_wrapper.cpp) rather than in Rust (handle.rs or lib.rs)
design_decisions.rationale: The contention is in OCCT's C++ global state, not in Rust. A C++ file-static std::mutex is the narrowest correct scope — it protects exactly the OCCT calls that share global state. Putting it in Rust would require a global Mutex visible across all OcctKernel instances, which is more invasive and less obviously correct (it would need to be in a static, adding lifetime concerns). The C++ mutex is invisible to Rust and requires no API changes.

design_decisions.decision: Use std::mutex (not a Rust-side serialization approach like a global channel)
design_decisions.rationale: Each OcctKernelHandle already has its own dedicated thread. We don't want to funnel all export work through a single global thread (that would be M4's architecture). A simple mutex serializes just the STEP export call, allowing other kernel operations (execute, query, tessellate) to remain fully parallel across handles. This is the minimal fix.

design_decisions.decision: Guard only export_step, not all OCCT FFI functions
design_decisions.rationale: Per the memory note (feedback_occt_thread_safety.md), BRep operations also use hidden global state. However, only STEP export is currently failing. The dedicated-thread pattern already serializes operations within each kernel instance. Cross-kernel contention on BRep ops hasn't been observed to fail because BRep operations touch less shared global state than STEP's Transfer/Write pipeline. If other operations start failing under concurrency, the same mutex pattern can be extended. For now, fix only what's broken.

design_decisions.decision: Add an explicit parallel-export regression test rather than relying on implicit test parallelism
design_decisions.rationale: The existing tests fail only because cargo test happens to run them in parallel. That's a fragile way to catch a race condition. An explicit test that spawns multiple handles and exports concurrently is a deterministic regression guard that documents the known concurrency hazard.

reuse.how: The new regression test reuses the existing spawn/execute/export API — no new Rust infrastructure needed. The fix is entirely in the C++ layer.
The new test follows the same pattern as export_step_contains_iso_header (spawn handle, create box, export, check ISO-10303-21) but in a parallel loop with multiple handles.
File-static std::mutex with std::lock_guard RAII pattern — standard C++ concurrency primitive, no new dependencies.
